title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 14
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: You have been asked to write a report for your town's planning committee about the state of the local parks. Mention the facilities for children, the safety of the areas, and recommend how to attract more people to use the parks.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report: Enhancing the Local Park Network

Introduction
The purpose of this report is to evaluate the current condition of our town’s public parks and to recommend improvements to increase their utility for the community. The assessment is based on observations and feedback from park users.

Facilities for Children
The existing playgrounds are, for the most part, functional but unimaginative. While the equipment in Jubilee Park is well-maintained, other areas, such as the North End Green, feature outdated metal structures that are poorly suited for younger children. There is a noticeable lack of inclusive play equipment for children with disabilities, which is a significant oversight that should be addressed in future budgets.

Safety and Maintenance
Safety remains a primary concern for many residents. In several parks, the perimeter fencing is damaged, and the lack of lighting in the evening makes these spaces feel unwelcoming after dark. Furthermore, the presence of litter and overgrown paths in the less central parks suggests that maintenance schedules are currently inadequate.

Recommendations for Improvement
To encourage higher attendance, I suggest:
- Modernisation: Replacing old equipment with 'nature-inspired' play areas and inclusive swings.
- Safety Lighting: Installing motion-sensor LED lighting along main pathways.
- Community Events: Organizing monthly 'park runs' or outdoor yoga sessions to foster a culture of active use.

Conclusion
Our parks are a vital asset for public health. By investing in better equipment and safety measures, the committee can ensure these spaces are used to their full potential by all citizens.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt: You have read an article in a national newspaper suggesting that all school leavers should be forced to do a year of community service before starting university. Write a letter to the editor expressing your views on this proposal.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I am writing in response to your recent article proposing a mandatory year of community service for all school leavers. While I admire the sentiment of fostering social responsibility, I believe that making such a scheme 'forced' is fundamentally counterproductive.

Firstly, the transition from school to adulthood is a pivotal time where young people must learn to make their own choices. Imposing a mandatory year of labour, however well-intentioned, could breed resentment towards the very sectors that need enthusiastic support. Volunteering is, by definition, a voluntary act; its value lies in the spirit of the individual’s commitment. Removing that choice devalues the essence of service.

Secondly, many students rely on the period between school and university to work and save money for their studies. In an era of rising tuition fees and living costs, a mandatory unpaid year would place an unfair financial burden on those from less privileged backgrounds, potentially widening the gap in educational equality.

Instead of a mandatory requirement, I would suggest that the government provides better incentives for a voluntary gap year. Offering university credit or financial grants to those who choose to serve in hospitals, schools, or environmental projects would attract those who are truly motivated to help, ensuring the quality of the service provided is high.

We should encourage our youth to give back to society, but we should do so through inspiration and support, not through legal compulsion.

Yours faithfully,

Clara Beaumont

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have been asked to write a review for a student magazine about a new sports or fitness centre in your area. Describe the facilities, the classes offered, and whether it represents good value for a student budget.
tasks.modelAnswer:
The Iron Hub: A New Era for Local Fitness?

As the latest addition to our local fitness scene, 'The Iron Hub' has generated significant buzz. I spent a week testing their facilities to see if it lives up to the hype, particularly for those of us on a tight student budget.

The facilities are undeniably impressive. The centre boasts a sprawling weight room with the latest resistance machines and a dedicated area for functional training, which is a refreshing change from the cramped basement gyms typical of the area. Everything is impeccably clean, and the changing rooms are spacious and well-ventilated. 

Regarding the classes, there is a commendable variety. From high-intensity interval training (HIIT) to restorative yoga, the instructors are professional and appear genuinely interested in participants' progress. I particularly enjoyed the 'Sunrise Cycle' class, which provided a great energy boost before my morning lectures. However, the classes do get booked up very quickly, so you need to be organised with the mobile app.

Is it good value for money? For students, the answer is a qualified 'yes'. While the standard membership is pricey, they offer a 'Student Off-Peak' pass which is 40% cheaper. If you can train before 4:00 PM, it is an absolute bargain. However, if you can only visit in the evenings, you might find the full price a bit steep compared to the university’s own sports hall.

In summary, if you’re serious about your training and can work around the peak hours, The Iron Hub is the best facility in town. It’s a professional environment that makes working out feel less like a chore and more like a hobby.
